Next we're going to look at something extremely important. Probably the most important thing in running meta ads is your metrics. So if you see here we're inside of a campaign. I just went in here and into the campaign and then you can see all these different columns and if I scroll over there's a few different things here. Quite a few things listed. Now we can control what we see here. We don't always want to see everything listed here. We may have an interest in a different metric that's not shown. So we go here to this arrow beside columns. Click on that and you can see all the different choices. These ones up here, all the way up to I would say there, these are already made by meta. They sit in the account and they're just there by default. So if I click on here and I go customize columns, you can see here there's all these different sections, engagement, page post, conversion, settings. Each one of these things has a set of selections that we can make, things that we can turn on and off. And the cool thing is we can also organize them. So you can slide here and change the order that I see them. I find that really handy and really important. And then the other thing we can do, I'll show this at the start and at the end is we can click this little box here and give it a name and save it as a preset so we don't lose this work. Every time we go into this add account, we can call up that preset and then go and view it. We don't have to change it every single time. So I also have a little cheat sheet here. I'm going to slide this over a little bit. And these are the columns that I like to set up. So if you see here on my left, you see budget, amount spent, results, cost for result, purchase, cost for purchase, outbound clicks, outbound CTR or click through rate, frequency, reach impressions, CPM or cost per thousand impressions, post engagement quality ranking, engagement rate ranking and conversion rate ranking. Now they're in that order for a reason. I like to group things together that matter as far as like if we see down here, post engagement, quality ranking and engagement rate and conversion. Those things there are all related. It doesn't make any sense to randomly throw the man amongst these others. If we look up here, for example, budget and amount spent, those are dollar related of course, results and cost per result. It makes sense to have those together. Purchase and cost per purchase. And then right here, if you picture a line going through here above this line, those are the metrics that I need to see ASAP right off the bat. As soon as I get into the campaign, that's what I want to see. And then if everything's in line there, I may not even need to look at these other things below that line. But if I do, let's say up top here, I'm not liking what I'm seeing or I'm curious, why is this one performing better than that one or worse than that one? Then it would start to look a little bit deeper. So let's say here, cost per purchase, everything below that is where we're looking. Imagine things aren't going super great. I might look at clicks. Are they actually clicking on things? What's the rate that they're clicking through? Frequency. How many times is each person seeing the ad reach? Is it even reaching enough people? Impressions. How many times is that ad being shown? Each impression, one impression is one time that the ad shows up in somebody's feed. Cost per thousand impressions. That's interesting to watch over time because if it's costing a fortune to get impressions, there may be something you need to look further into. Post engagement, are people actually responding to the ad and quality ranking? These four down here, post engagement, quality ranking, engagement, and conversion rate ranking, those have to do with the quality of the ad and how the audience is responding to that ad. Meta wants us to create good ads that people are interested in, things that don't upset them. We don't want to have people clicking away on our ad or being upset by our ad. If they're happy with it, these four metrics at the bottom, they should be positive. Let's look a little further into this. We see here, keeping this cheat sheet in mind on the left hand side, if we look on the right hand side here, you see delivery ads that name, bid strategy. You can go through here and type these things in. Let's say I said, budget's already up there. Let's just remove budget. If I wanted to add that, I'd simply type in budget and then I'd click on budget. You can find it down at the bottom here. What I find way easier to do when you're first setting this up, just remove everything. Remove everything. Then we'll say, first thing is budget. You simply click budget, then there it's at the top. Then we do the same thing for amount spent. There it is. As you're going through these and typing them in, more than one might come up. I started typing results. There was result. Then cost per result was right below it. The order that you click them in is the order that will appear on your right hand side here. Another one that's interesting here is purchase. Let's do this one. Purchase. You can see a whole bunch of things come up here. We've got a bunch of custom purchases, I guess you'd say, in our account here. You might just have purchase and then cost per purchase. You'll see all these different things come up, mobile app purchase, website purchase offline. I just deselect all of these. We're not running ads to an app or anything like that. I just want to see the overall purchases. Another one that I want to talk about is clicks. Let's say I go in here and I type in clicks. You see clicks all. You definitely don't want that. That would be anything at all that they click on your ad. They like it, they comment, they share. Those all would be included under this sort of clicks. That's not what we want. Link clicks is closer to the truth. Depending on where your ad is going, in this example that we've been working on here, we're sending people off of Meta onto a landing page to register for a webinar. That's an outbound click. These will be very similar. If I choose link clicks and outbound clicks, they'll be super, super close. The more accurate one in my case here is outbound clicks. Then the next thing you want to do is find out your cost per click. You want to make sure they match. I would choose cost per outbound click. We'll go and add all the others. There we go. Now I've added everything. You can see them all over here on the right. Right down to conversion rate ranking. Now this part, like I said, is very important. I mentioned at the start. You want to click save as preset. In this case, I'm going to call it, you can call it whatever you want. I'm going to call this GC dashboard. Like I said, you can call it anything you want. The name should mean something to you. If you've got a special dashboard set up, say just for engagement or just for purchases, you would name it accordingly. Just has to be something that you'd remember. Then you click apply. If you've already created the thing, it's going to say do you want to replace this? Just choose replace. Here we go. Now you can see, here's all our columns, delivery. Actually, let's make sure we're in the right thing here. See it went to the default. Now down here, we'll go to GC dashboard. They should change. There we go. So now we've got budget, amount spent, attribution setting. It shows that you can turn that off, but you actually can't turn that off. It'll always come back. Results, cost per result, purchase, cost per purchase, outbound clicks, cost per outbound click, frequency impressions, CPM cost per thousand impressions, and post engagement. Now you might be thinking, hey, that's not where we stopped. Where are the others? Well, we're in the ad set level and the other two two or three that are missing here are actually in the ad level. So if I pick one of these, doesn't matter which one, we'll just pick that one. Now we're in the ads and if I scroll over here, you can see post engagement where we stopped before, quality ranking, engagement rate ranking, and conversion rate ranking. Now these, I would take these a little bit with a grain of salt, but you want them to be average or above average. If these things are poor, they used to have, these call these just the relevant score. And if your ad is not relevant or low quality, and it continues to run on and on and on, it will just hamper performance. Performance will be poor because it's an ad that people aren't responding well to and they just simply don't want to see it. So if you can have it be above average, that is the goal. And this is why we check in on our metrics. So while we're in here, let's just look at a few of these. So you can see I've got a bunch of ad sets running here. They're running to different audiences and they're split testing different things, but they're all at the same budget. So if I go in here and I click on one of these headings, so results, let's just say we looked at our top three. You might choose these top three and you can click up here and search and filter and choose filter by selection. Now we'll only see those three. It makes it a lot easier to see what you're looking at as far as checking out your metrics. So now we can see these three over the last seven days. That one spent $450, $448, $447. So they're all very similar. 71 leads, 70 leads, 70 leads, again very similar. Cost per result is all very close on average $638. But purchases, this middle one has gotten two purchases. Now this account is trying to get people to register for a webinar. That's the target. But it's nice to see purchases. You can see some purchases have come through here. And actually I should also point out I added in this cost per purchase column because I like to see, you know, this spent $448. So we got two purchases. This just does the math for me. If you have, you know, 37 purchases here, you don't have to take that number and divide it. You can just see right here what your cost per purchase actually is. And then outbound clicks, they're clicking more on this AdSat 16. Not by a huge amount, but by a little bit. Cost per outbound click is pretty similar, not terribly different. You know, if this was like $5 and these were $240, that would be a different story. Frequency is about the same, very similar. Frequency is how often one person is seeing that ad on average. So this is like 5, 6, 7. That's getting crazy and people will get annoyed and that's when you'll start to get negative reactions on your ads. I'd say around two or less between one and two is probably ideal. And then impressions. This one down here, AS1 is kind of lagging, right? So it's doing really well. I would say like you can't just determine everything off of one metric. So these each got 20,000 impressions, but this only got 13,000. But if we scroll over, it's in line with the other two as far as how many leads it got and its cost per lead. Coming back over here, if you find the cost per thousand impressions is extremely out. Now these are a little bit of a distance, but let's say these were 20 bucks per thousand impressions and this was 50 or 60, that might be cause for concern. Because even though they're close, you might want to turn this one off and let the allot that budget to these other two. And then we see post-engagement. So they're all very similar that way. Now we'll leave these three selected. I was going to just click on the ads heading here. You could select these just one and go look at its ads all by itself, but I'm going to look at all of them together. And in that case, we would just hit columns again. We go customize and here we go ad set, ad set name and save it as our preset. Actually, let's move it around a bit first. We'll leave it there just so I know where it is. Ad set preset and apply, replace. And now when I go into these, I can see, I scroll over here, I can see what ad set they're in. So this is in ad set one, 16 and 10. So if I'm looking at each of those, now I know where to go and go and look at them if I want to edit them individually. Very, very handy editing of these columns.
